title: How to Work with Body Memories
researcherTopic: Internal Family Systems, parts work, Self-energy
metaDescription: Explore how body memories hold the unprocessed story of trauma and learn how to work with them through somatic awareness, IFS, and gentle presence.
imageDescription: A piece of broken pottery is being meticulously repaired with gold, following the Japanese art of kintsugi. Warm, soft light illuminates the scene, highlighting the golden cracks as a beautiful part of the object's history. The overall feeling is one of serene, gentle restoration and healing.
htmlContent:
The scent of rain on hot asphalt. Not the gentle, cleansing rain of a spring afternoon, but the sudden, violent downpour of a summer storm that cracks the sky open. There is no memory attached, no story, just the raw, unfiltered language of the body speaking a truth the mind has long since learned to ignore. This is the terrain of body memories ~ the echoes of experience held not in narrative, but in the very tissues of our being.

The Unspoken Archive of the Nervous System

We tend to think of memory as a cinematic experience, a reel of images and sounds we can play back in the theater of the mind. Yet for those of us who have navigated the complex territory of trauma, memory is often a far more visceral, fragmented, and deeply physical affair. It arrives not as a story, but as a sensation... These are body memories, the imprints of overwhelming experience that were never properly processed and encoded into a coherent narrative by the higher-order brain structures. Instead, they remain stored as raw, unintegrated sensory data, lodged in the nervous system. These are not memories we *have*; they are experiences the body is perpetually *living*.

The Brain as a Prediction Machine

To truly grasp why body memories wield such a powerful influence, we must appreciate the fundamental nature of the brain. It is not a passive receiver of information; it is an active and relentless prediction engine. This predictive process is incredibly efficient, saving us the immense cognitive load of figuring everything out from scratch in every moment. It’s why you can catch a ball without calculating its trajectory.

When the database of past experiences is heavily weighted with trauma, the prediction machinery becomes exquisitely biased toward threat. The nervous system begins to operate from a non-conscious assumption of danger, interpreting neutral stimuli... a certain tone of voice, a flickering light - as precursors to harm. It turns out to be not a cognitive error; it is a somatic one. This is why trying to "think" our way out of a body memory is so often a futile exercise. We cannot reason with a system that is operating on a logic far older and faster than reason itself. The work is not to argue with the prediction, but to offer the nervous system new, corrective experiences that slowly update its predictive model. We can learn how the nervous system learns from the past and begin to offer it a different future.

Meeting the Messengers Within: An IFS Perspective

One of the most compassionate and effective frameworks for this delicate work comes from Internal Family Systems (IFS), a model developed by researcher Dick Schwartz. IFS proposes that our psyche is not a monolithic entity but is naturally comprised of a multiplicity of "parts" - sub-personalities holding our beliefs, emotions, and memories. To protect the system from the flood of these exiled feelings, other parts step in to become powerful "protectors."

A tool that many find helpful for releasing stored body tension is TriggerPoint GRID Foam Roller (paid link).

These protectors come in two main varieties: proactive "managers" who try to control our lives to avoid triggers, and reactive "firefighters" who jump into action with numbing or distracting behaviors when an exile is triggered. Body memories are often the language of these exiled parts, the only way they know how to communicate their distress. The sudden tightness in the chest is a young part crying out for help. a core of calm, compassionate, curious, and connected consciousness. By approaching these internal messengers with curiosity, we can unburden them of the sensations they carry. What we find here is the essence of healing the fragmented self ~ not through force, but through compassionate internal diplomacy.

Bear with me on this one.

The First Steps: Titration and Pendulation

How do we begin this delicate dance with the body's wisdom? The key is to proceed with immense gentleness. Two of the most important principles in somatic work are *titration* and *pendulation*. Pendulation is the practice of gently swinging the attention between the sensation of difficulty and a place in the body that feels neutral or resourced. This rhythmic movement prevents us from getting stuck in the vortex of the trauma and reinforces the nervous system's capacity for regulation.

A practical way to begin is to first find your anchor. Sit comfortably and let your awareness find a place in your body that feels relatively calm ~ perhaps your hands resting on your lap or your feet on the floor. Spend a few moments resting your attention there. Then, you might gently bring to mind a situation that creates a mild charge. Instead, you acknowledge it, and then gently guide your attention back to your anchor. You swing back and forth, spending most of your time in the place of resource. This communicates to the nervous system that we can approach these difficult places without being consumed by them. It is a foundational skill for anyone seeking to understand the window of tolerance and how to gently expand it.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link).

The Grammar of Embodiment

If body memories are a language, then embodiment is the practice of learning its grammar. It is the patient, moment-by-moment work of turning our attention away from the chatter of the mind and toward the direct, felt experience of the body. What emerges is not about analyzing the sensation or forcing it to change. We might notice its texture - is it sharp or dull, buzzing or heavy? We might notice its location, its shape, its subtle shifts. We are not looking for a dramatic release; we are simply becoming intimate with the question the sensation is asking.

This simple practice directly challenges the nervous system's predictive habits. When a familiar, trauma-related sensation arises and we meet it not with panic or dissociation but with calm presence, we are creating new data. A client once described this as "sitting with the ghost until it remembers it's dead." We are allowing the echo of the past to finally be heard and integrated, rather than endlessly replayed as a present reality. What we find here is a crucial step in understanding what a trauma response truly is and how it can be resolved.

The Breath as Companion

A simple and deeply effective entry point into this practice is through the breath. Not by controlling it or forcing it into a particular rhythm. The invitation is far more gentle: to treat the breath as a companion. Feeling the air move in and out, the gentle expansion of the ribcage, the subtle rise and fall of the abdomen. The breath is always in the present moment, a reliable anchor to the here and now.

By anchoring our attention to the physical sensations of breathing, we provide a safe harbor for the mind when the seas of somatic memory churn. It becomes a place to rest when the stories become too loud or the sensations too intense. The breath becomes the thread that leads us back out of the labyrinth of the past, a reminder that even when everything feels chaotic, this simple, vital process continues.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link).

The Shift Underneath

Working with body memories is a process of return to the wholeness that was never truly lost, only obscured. It involves gently and methodically rebuilding the bridge between mind and body, a connection that trauma so often severs. What emerges is not a linear process, and it is not always comfortable. This is to be expected, and it is not a sign of failure. The architecture of our survival strategies was built over years; the architecture of our return must be given the same patience and respect.

Wild, right?

Ultimately, where we place our attention determines the world we inhabit. We can keep our attention locked in the stories of the mind, or we can learn to anchor it in the living, breathing reality of the present moment. By choosing the latter, we do not erase the past, but we refuse to let it be the sole author of our future. We learn to inhabit our bodies not as haunted houses, but as wise and trusted homes, capable of holding the full spectrum of human experience with courage and wisdom. The body, once a source of terror, becomes a source of truth, a compass pointing not toward what was, but toward what is possible now.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can body memories go away on their own?

While their intensity may fade, body memories typically do not resolve on their own because they are stored as unprocessed survival responses. Without actively engaging with them and providing the nervous system with new, safe experiences, they tend to persist. The work is about integration, not waiting for them to disappear; it requires a conscious turning toward the sensation to allow the trapped survival energy to complete its cycle.

A body tool that some find helpful for releasing chronic tension is Acupressure Mat and Pillow Set (paid link).

Is this the same as a panic attack?

While a panic attack is a powerful somatic experience, it is not the same as a body memory. A panic attack is an overwhelming surge of the sympathetic nervous system, often with catastrophic thoughts. A body memory can be much more subtle... a chronic tightness, a specific pain... and is a direct sensory echo of a past traumatic event. A body memory can lead to a panic attack if met with fear, but the memory itself is the initial sensory signal.

Do I need a therapist to work with body memories?

While foundational practices of mindfulness can be explored safely on one's own, going deeper into the resolution of traumatic body memories is best done with a trauma-informed somatic therapist. A skilled practitioner provides a crucial co-regulating presence, helping your nervous system navigate intense sensations without becoming overwhelmed. They create a relational field of safety that allows the most vulnerable parts to finally feel secure enough to emerge and heal.
